page: OVHcloud d2-2 is an entry-level virtual server instance belonging to the Discovery family, utilizing the KVM hypervisor. It is configured with a single shared x86_64 vCPU based on an Intel Core Processor (Haswell architecture) running at 2.0 GHz. The hardware profile includes 2.0 GB of system memory and a local 25 GB NVMe SSD for storage. Network connectivity includes one complimentary public IPv4 address, though baseline network bandwidth is rated at 0 Gbps.

Benchmark results place the d2-2 in the bottom 10% across all evaluated performance metrics. The single-core and multi-core stress-ng CPU performance scores are both 850.47, reflecting the shared nature of its single physical core. Memory bandwidth is limited, with read-only large block transfers measuring 8.34 GB/sec and write operations at 7.57 GB/sec. Database and web serving capabilities are similarly constrained, with Redis SET operations executing at 0.96 million operations per second and static web serving throughput reaching 0.97 GB/sec.

Due to its shared CPU allocation and modest resource footprint, the d2-2 offers a highly cost-efficient entry point for non-production environments. It is best suited for lightweight workloads such as basic web serving, software development testing, personal projects, or learning environments where high-performance compute and high-speed network throughput are not required.
description: OVHcloud d2-2 is a budget-oriented virtual server from the Discovery family, running on the KVM hypervisor. It features a single shared x86_64 Intel Core Processor (Haswell) clocked at 2.0 GHz, paired with 2.0 GB of RAM and a 25 GB NVMe SSD. Performance benchmarks place this instance in the bottom 10% tier, with a single-core stress-ng score of 850.47 and a Redis SET throughput of 0.96 million operations per second. Given its shared CPU allocation and low resource density, the d2-2 is a highly cost-effective option for non-demanding, entry-level workloads. It is best suited for basic web serving, development testing, and lightweight personal projects rather than production databases or intensive computational tasks.
